perturbation.original_text: 3. Non-Disclosure Agreements from Third Parties. If Disclosing Party gives its written consent to Recipient to disclose Information or Items to a third party, Recipient shall require the third party to execute a non-disclosure agreement in form, and substance satisfactory to Disclosing Party. Recipient shall promptly furnish Disclosing Party with a copy of all such non-disclosure agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Non-Disclosure Agreements from Third Parties. Recipient is strictly prohibited from disclosing any Information or Items to any third party, regardless of whether the Disclosing Party gives written consent.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for disclosure to third parties with the Disclosing Party's consent and the execution of a non-disclosure agreement. The modified text outright prohibits disclosure to third parties, regardless of consent. This is a direct contradiction that removes the possibility of third-party disclosure under any circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Non-Disclosure Agreements from Third Parties. Recipient is strictly prohibited from disclosing any Information or Items to any third party, regardless of whether the Disclosing Party gives written consent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3 outright prohibits disclosure to third parties under any circumstance, regardless of consent from the Disclosing Party. The contradicted text in Section 3 is exactly the same, but the original text in Section 3 allowed disclosure to third parties with written consent and a non-disclosure agreement. Since the changed text now prohibits something that was previously allowed under specific conditions, this creates a direct in-text contradiction, fitting the definition of structural flaws by placing the contradicting clause in the same location. Therefore, the answer is YES.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Only One Source of Discussion. You agree that you will only discuss the Matter with Mr. Al Weber, Chief Executive Officer, or Mr. Thomas V. Gilboy, Chief Financial Officer of the Disclosing Party. Under no circumstances will you discuss the Matter, or admit that the Matter has been a topic of discussion with any other employees of the Disclosing Party, or any other third party. In addition, during 2000, you agree not to undertake any unusual efforts to research the business, operations, prospects or finances of the Disclosing Party, where such research efforts would include discussions with any third party, except in the ordinary course of business.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Only One Source of Discussion. You agree that you will discuss the Matter with any employees or third parties of your choosing. Under no circumstances are you limited on who you discuss the matter with.
perturbation.explanation: This change directly opposes the original restriction. The original text strictly limited discussions to specific individuals. The modified text allows discussions with anyone, thus completely negating the original intent and creating significant ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Only One Source of Discussion. You agree that you will discuss the Matter with any employees or third parties of your choosing. Under no circumstances are you limited on who you discuss the matter with.
perturbation.justification: The original text limits discussions to specific individuals (Mr. Al Weber, Chief Executive Officer, or Mr. Thomas V. Gilboy, Chief Financial Officer), while the changed text allows discussions with any employees or third parties without limitation. The contradicted text is a copy of the Changed Text. This directly contradicts the original intent of limiting discussions and creates ambiguity about who the user is allowed to discuss the matter with, therefore there is a contradiction. The Perturbation Type is 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction', reinforcing that the location of these contradicting texts in multiple locations in the contract creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Employees. For a period of two (2) years Recipient (or any agent or affiliates of the Recipient) will not have discussions with any "Covered Employees" of the Disclosing Party regarding employment opportunities with the Recipient or any of its affiliates. "Covered Employees" will include any person who is employed by the recipient as a part or full time employee either as of the date of this Agreement, or persons that subsequently become employees of the Disclosing Party. However, if a person subsequently terminates their employment with the Disclosing Party, then the recipient can have such discussions with that person, after a 5-day waiting period. In addition, if the Recipient published a general posting for employment opportunities in any publication or web site, and one of the Recipient's Covered Employees should respond to the posting, then the Recipient will be free to have such discussions with that person.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Employees. The Recipient can discuss employment opportunities with any employee of the disclosing party, despite them being a 'Covered Employee'.
perturbation.explanation: This change negates the original restrictions on discussing employment with "Covered Employees". By explicitly stating that the Recipient can have discussions with any employee, it directly contradicts the two-year restriction and creates confusion over the Recipient's obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Employees. The Recipient can discuss employment opportunities with any employee of the disclosing party, despite them being a 'Covered Employee'.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the Recipient can discuss employment opportunities with any employee, despite them being a 'Covered Employee'. This directly contradicts the original restriction on discussing employment with 'Covered Employees' for a period of two years. Since the Contradicted Text and Changed Text are the same, it highlights that the change overwrites the restriction from the original text, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. No Proprietary Right; Return of Information or Items. Nothing herein shall confer upon Recipient any right, title, interest or license whatsoever in or to any Information or Items. At the end of our discussions regarding the Matter, the Disclosing Party has the right to request that the Recipient return all Information or Items to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. No Proprietary Right; Return of Information or Items. Recipient shall have perpetual ownership over any Information or Items disclosed and is not obligated to return them to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that the Recipient gains no proprietary rights and must return the information if requested. The modified text grants perpetual ownership to the Recipient and removes the obligation to return the information. This represents a direct contradiction regarding ownership and return obligations. The contradicted_text, in essence, is the same location where the inserted perturbation happens. This change of pertubation creates ambiguity over the rights of the items
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. No Proprietary Right; Return of Information or Items. Recipient shall have perpetual ownership over any Information or Items disclosed and is not obligated to return them to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the recipient gains no ownership rights and must return information if requested. The changed text grants perpetual ownership and removes the return obligation. The contradicted text is the changed text, and therefore, the contradiction is that the recipient now has ownership and no return obligation, which is directly opposite of the original terms. This creates a clear in-text contradiction as defined by the Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction definition: "Structural flaws also cause in-text contradictions when a clause is placed in multiple locations with conflicting implications."
